project.name: Forsetti Framework
project.summary: A reusable, adaptable, modular Swift/Apple-framework runtime for consistent app development across iOS and macOS. Apps discover, validate, unlock, and activate bundled modules. Forsetti is modularity-first and Apple-native by design: app-owned modules should use Apple-native technologies in the correct architectural layer. In multi-module single applications, the app UI lives in a dedicated UI module that may use SwiftUI; Apple-native frameworks such as Metal are valid where appropriate to a module's responsibility.
principles: Strict modularity: modules are self-contained and do not depend on other modules.

Boundary-mediated architecture: shared contracts, events, services, and routing should flow through Forsetti-defined boundaries; do not create improper cross-layer coupling or ad-hoc host dependencies.
iOS-compliant: no runtime loading of new native code; all modules ship in the app bundle and are unlocked via IAP.

For single applications composed of multiple modules, exactly one dedicated UI module is active at a time alongside service modules. That UI module may use SwiftUI and other Apple-native UI frameworks/tools. Single-module apps include their own UI inside the module and run silently; no separate UI module is needed.
In Forsetti documentation, host UI refers to the application UI used by the app built on Forsetti. In the multi-module single-application pattern, this UI belongs in exactly one dedicated UI module.
Framework-internal target restrictions protect package layering and are not blanket bans on Apple-native frameworks in app-owned modules.
Use Apple-native frameworks in the correct module/layer, including Metal where rendering/graphics responsibilities require it.

swift_rules: Use #if os(iOS)/#if os(macOS) for platform adapters where necessary.
Expose a unified ForsettiRuntime.platform enum used by core logic.
Do not place platform-branching logic in shared/core module logic. Keep platform-specific branching in the appropriate platform or app-owned layer.

layers.name: ForsettiCore
layers.role: Stable public surface: protocols, manifests, compatibility, lifecycle, activation, routing overlay model, event bus interfaces, capability model, diagnostics.

layers.name: ForsettiPlatform
layers.role: OS adapters: networking/auth/storage/secure storage/file system/UI hosting implementations. Hides iOS/macOS differences from modules.

layers.name: ForsettiHostTemplate
layers.role: Developer-facing app template for module discovery, activation, and testing. Includes framework controls (Home, Settings, module switcher) that are intended for use during development. In production single-module apps, these controls are hidden and the framework runs silently behind the module's own UI. This host/template surface is a reference implementation and does not imply that SwiftUI is framework-internal only.

layers.name: ForsettiModules
layers.role: Bundled module targets. Each module includes a manifest and a factory, conforms to ForsettiModule, ForsettiAppModule, or ForsettiUIModule.

delivery.iOS: All modules compiled into app; discovered at runtime via manifests; unlocked via IAP; activated/deactivated at runtime; app restart acceptable.
delivery.macOS: Same as iOS for v1; design allows later adding external plugin bundle sources.

types.service_module: Adds capabilities/services; no UI overlay required. Used in multi-module applications for individual features.
types.ui_module: Provides UI overlay/mask + optional toolbar items + optional view injections + overlay routing schema. Used in multi-module single applications as the dedicated UI module. Exactly one UI module active at a time alongside any number of active service modules. The dedicated UI module may implement application UI using SwiftUI and other Apple-native UI technologies; service modules remain separate from the UI module.
types.app_module: A complete single-application module (ForsettiAppModule) that includes its own UI. The framework runs silently; end users see only the module's UI. Updates are delivered by swapping the module. This is the most common module type for production apps.

recommended.package_manager: Swift Package Manager (preferred) or Xcode workspace with frameworks
notes: Keep ForsettiCore independent of UI/platform frameworks. Place SwiftUI and other Apple-native UI code in the appropriate app-owned UI or host layer; prefer platform-neutral models in Core.
Apply Apple-native frameworks such as Metal in the correct owning module/layer when responsibilities require rendering, graphics, or compute.
ForsettiPlatform may import UIKit/AppKit as needed.

compatibility_rules: Compatibility passes if min <= ForsettiCore.version and (max absent or ForsettiCore.version <= max).

protocols.name: ForsettiModule
notes: start must be idempotent; if called twice, it must no-op or restart cleanly.
stop must remove all framework registrations (events, injections, toolbar, overlays).

protocols.name: ForsettiAppModule
notes: A complete single-application module that includes its own UI.
Use for simple applications where the entire app (UI + logic) lives in one module.
The framework runs silently in the background; end users interact only with the module's UI.
Updates are delivered by swapping the module. Module manifest uses moduleType: app.

protocols.name: ForsettiUIModule
constraints: For multi-module single applications, framework enforces exactly one active UI module at a time alongside any number of service modules.
UIContributions must be overlays/masks; never replace app root view. This target-level rule is not a global ban on SwiftUI in app-owned UI modules.

protocols.name: ForsettiEntitlementProvider

methods: isUnlocked(moduleID: String, productID: String?) async -> Bool

notes: iOS implementation backed by StoreKit (v2 preferred).
macOS implementation may be stub/internal license list for v1.

protocols.name: ForsettiEventBus
notes: Modules may only communicate via EventBus and framework-defined services.

types_and_models.name: ModuleManifest
fields: entryPoint:String (symbolic name used by registry/factory mapping)

types_and_models.name: OverlaySchema
types_and_models.purpose: Defines pointers/paths to reach existing base navigation destinations and introduces overlay routes without changing root stack.

types_and_models.name: BaseDestinationRef
types_and_models.purpose: A stable identifier for a destination in the base app navigation.

types_and_models.name: OverlayRoute
types_and_models.purpose: Optional extra route that maps to an existing base destination or to a module-provided overlay screen hosted within allowed slots.

types_and_models.name: ViewInjectionDescriptor
types_and_models.purpose: Injects module-provided SwiftUI views into predefined host slots.

runtime_components_to_implement.name: ForsettiRuntime
responsibilities: Provide a single entrypoint to boot Forsetti in host app.

runtime_components_to_implement.name: ModuleRegistry
responsibilities: List all compiled-in module factories (manual list for v1).
notes: v1: implement a static registry file developers edit when adding a module.
future: generate registry at build time.

runtime_components_to_implement.name: ManifestLoader
responsibilities: Load all ModuleManifest JSON files from a known bundle location.

runtime_components_to_implement.name: CompatibilityChecker
responsibilities: Check UI module activation policy for multi-module applications (one UI module active per single application).

runtime_components_to_implement.name: ModuleManager
responsibilities: Instantiate modules via ModuleRegistry only when needed.

state_model.enabledServiceModuleIDs: Set<String> — all active service modules (multiple allowed simultaneously).
state_model.enabledUIModuleIDs: Set<String> — compatibility state for the active UI/app module. The default runtime model keeps this set empty or containing exactly one module ID.
state_model.activeUIModuleID: String? — the active UI/app module. Activating another UI/app module deactivates the previous one; service modules remain concurrently active.

runtime_components_to_implement.name: ActivationStore
responsibilities: Persist enabled modules and active UI module across launches.

storage.secure: Keychain for sensitive settings if needed

runtime_components_to_implement.name: CapabilityPolicy
responsibilities: Define which capabilities are allowed per app build/config.
Optionally require user consent prompts mediated by ForsettiPlatform.

runtime_components_to_implement.name: UISurfaceManager
responsibilities: Maintain active view injections by slot.
Expose SwiftUI-friendly publishers/observable objects for the host UI to render overlays.

runtime_components_to_implement.name: ForsettiContext
responsibilities: Provide a restricted interface surface to modules.
Expose event bus, routing overlay APIs, logging, platform services (abstracted).
runtime_components_to_implement.rule: No UIKit/AppKit references in ForsettiContext public API.

entitlement_model: Each module may specify an iapProductID in its manifest.
EntitlementProvider checks current entitlements and caches unlocked moduleIDs.
Entitlements changes trigger ModuleManager eligibility updates.
implementation_tasks: Implement StoreKit2 receipt/transaction checking for product IDs.
Provide a simple async isUnlocked(productID) API.
Provide a way to restore purchases.

macos.v1: Stub provider with local allowlist; keep protocol compatible with iOS.

base_navigation.definition: Host app defines a fixed set of BaseDestinationRef destinations; modules can only point to these.

requirements: Host root view consumes UISurfaceManager observable state.
Slots render injected views by priority (highest wins) or stacking rules.
ThemeMask tokens map to host Theme system (host decides how tokens apply).
Toolbar items rendered from descriptors, actions routed back through Forsetti.

module_isolation: Modules may not import other module targets.
Modules may not call OS APIs directly for privileged operations; must request via Forsetti services.
Modules communicate only via ForsettiEventBus and Forsetti-defined services.
oo_guidelines: SwiftUI Views may be structs; design should follow OO principles via composition, encapsulation, single responsibility.
Business logic should live in class-based services/controllers/view-models as appropriate.

deliverables: ForsettiCore package with SemVer, Platform, Capability, protocols, manifests models.
HostTemplate app that lists modules and allows enable/disable and UI module selection.
Persist activation state; restart optional but not required for service modules.
ExampleServiceModule: publishes events, uses storage abstraction.
ExampleUIModule: provides theme mask + toolbar items + overlay pointers + one injected view.
API surface audit: mark ForsettiCore public/stable vs internal.

acceptance_criteria: On iOS, app boots, discovers all bundled manifests, and lists modules.
Locked modules are visible but cannot be activated until unlocked via IAP provider.
Single-module app: the ForsettiAppModule (with its own UI) is active and the framework runs silently in the background.
Multi-module single application: all service modules and exactly one UI module are active simultaneously.
Developer-testing context: one module (application) is active at a time, switchable via framework controls.
Deactivating a module removes its UI overlays and stops event subscriptions/services.
Modules can add navigation pointers to base destinations without altering base navigation stack.
Modules cannot depend on other modules; communication occurs only through ForsettiEventBus.
implementation_notes: Implement v1 with a static ModuleRegistry (manual list) to keep things simple; add codegen later if desired.
Use Swift Concurrency (async/await) for entitlement checks and lifecycle where helpful.
Avoid UIKit/AppKit in ForsettiCore; keep platform-specific code in ForsettiPlatform.
Keep public protocols minimal and stable; everything else can evolve.
